narrative: As an Asian male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 36, you brought some strong qualifications to your law school application, including plans to attend full-time, which is the norm for most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 56.8%—just over the threshold that led to your rejection. This prediction was driven primarily by three key factors from your profile: your LSAT score, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.50. Your LSAT was the most influential positive factor, as it was close to the average of 36.7 and pushed the model's thinking toward expecting you to pass, showing your potential in that area. In contrast, your Year 3 decile—well below the typical 5.53 average—had the strongest negative pull, signaling to the model that your later academic performance ranked low among peers and raising concerns about bar exam readiness. Your undergraduate GPA, at 2.50 compared to the average 3.21, also contributed toward the failure prediction, reinforcing doubts about your overall academic track record. Compared to typical students, your lower-middle family income bracket and early Year 1 decile of 2 similarly placed you below averages, though they played smaller roles here. The model weighs these indicators heavily because past data shows they strongly correlate with first-time bar success across diverse applicants like yourself. While your LSAT gave you an edge over many, the gaps in grades tipped the balance toward rejection. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT if possible to boost it further, or focus on improving your GPA through post-baccalaureate courses or a master's program to lift those decile rankings. Building a stronger narrative around your Asian background—perhaps highlighting resilience or targeted prep—could also help in reapplying to schools with more holistic reviews. With targeted improvements, you can shift this prediction in your favor next time.
